section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT PRICE ACTION MODEL 2: SHORT-TERM TRADING PLAN
content:
Welcome to Price Action Model Number Two: Short-Term Trading Plan. The objective for this model is 50 to
100 Pips per trade. This model, like the first, will feature repetitive slides, but the logic submitted in each
trade plan holds crucial differences that require close attention.

The standard process for every trade plan involves several key stages:
- Preparation Stage
- Opportunity Discovery
- Trade Planning
- Trade Execution
- Trade Management
PREPARATION:

In the preparation stage, we always refer to the economic calendar for the coming week. We note all
medium and high-impact news events for the markets we intend to follow. We study the events on the week
to come and consider how the current market structure and calendar events may suggest a specific weekly
profile for that week's range.
In the simplest way, we look for a collection out of the weekly templates that are bullish when we are
anticipating the market to go higher. By comparing the bullish templates with the calendar, we can match
up the likely weekly profile. For example, if a high-impact news event is centered on Tuesday London open,
we look at the Tuesday low of the week weekly profile and/or the Wednesday low of the week weekly profile.
PREPARATION:

section_title: DETERMINING THE EPOD DATA RANGE (DEALING RANGE)
content:
For preparation, we determine the EPOD data range for the last 20 days, excluding Sundays. We note the
highest high and the lowest low of the past 20 days; this will be your current dealing range. If today is a
certain date, we look back 20 days from today, and that trading day's highest high and lowest low define
our dealing range.

section_title: EXTENDING THE DEALING RANGE LOOKBACK AND MARKET SELECTION
content:
Sometimes you may need to look back 40 days or 60 days when there are no clear PD (Price Delivery)
arrays to choose from in the last 20 days. This occurs if we have already worked inside the last 20 days'
dealing range, both up and down.
- If the 20-day range is exhausted, use a 40-day lookback.
- If the 40-day range is exhausted, use a 60-day lookback.
- If no clear and logical PD array can be found within a 60-day lookback, then choose another market to
trade or refrain from trading.

section_title: IDENTIFYING THE NEXT DRAW IN LIQUIDITY AND PD ARRAY DIRECTION
content:
Within this dealing range, we look for the next draw in liquidity. Where is price likely to trade to next? An old
High/low, a fair value gap, or a liquidity pool. We look for a PD array in the direction of the weekly range
bias:
- Premium PD arrays when bullish.
- Discount arrays when bearish.
We anticipate price to move to a PD array that would support our weekly bias on a day and economic
calendar event found on the economic calendar within the current or next trading week. This is what we
wait for every time we sit down in front of the charts. We are not looking for something to magically pop up;
we are looking for something that fits this narrative. If it doesn't present itself, then we have no trade. This
can either be a run on liquidity or a rebalancing of inefficiency in price.

Opportunity Discovery involves identifying specific price action around key times:
- Identify discount arrays under Tuesday's European opening price inside the 20-day IPA data range when
the bias is bullish.
- Identify premium arrays above Tuesday's European open inside the 20-day if the data range when the
bias is bearish.
- Identify discount arrays under Wednesday's European opening price inside the 20-day if the data range
when the bias is bullish.
- Identify premium arrays above Wednesday's European opening price inside the 20-day if the data range
when the bias is bearish.
Note: If the signal or setup does not form on a Tuesday, we would still consider it on a Wednesday.

When the market is primed, we want to look for a convergence of manipulation in price opposite to our
trade bias, at a time the economic calendar suggests a volatility injection will likely unfold. What we are
looking for is some snappy, sharp price movement that would be in the opposite direction, trading into our
predetermined level of entry. This is basically a Judas swing.

We are looking for a period of market protraction that is opposed to our trade direction moving in profit. In
other words, we want to see price shoot up when we are looking to go short, and we want to see it shoot
down when we want to go long. We are not chasing price.
- We will short above the European opening price.
- We will buy below the European opening price.

When we are bearish, we will frame a short entry when the price has moved up into a 15-minute premium
PD array that converges with a standard deviation of no more than three standard deviations.
- The plus three indicates that if you are using the Asian range, Central Bank dealers range, or flout, you
are only going up three standard deviations; nothing higher than that for your entry.
- This setup will typically form during London open or New York open.
- The market will likely create the weekly low during the Thursday New York session. For time filter and
targeting purposes, we will anticipate the trade to pan out until that time.

When we are bullish, we will frame a long entry when price has moved down into a 15-minute discount PD
array that converges with a standard deviation of no more than three standard deviations lower.
- Nothing lower than three Asian range standard deviations projected lower, or three Central Bank dealers
range projected lower, or three flout levels lower.
- This setup will typically form during London open or New York open.
- The market will likely create the weekly high during the Thursday New York session. For time filter and
targeting purposes, we will anticipate the trade to pan out until that time.

When we are bearish, we will target the sell side liquidity below an old daily low or fair value gap inside the
20-day IPA data range, converging with a negative or projected lower standard deviation. This means if we
are short or getting ready to take a short, what are our targets?
- We are trying to predict the Thursday or sooner range that would offer 50 to 100 Pips.
- Our target has to be in the form of a discount array if we are bearish.
- We are looking for a standard deviation lower that converges both with a standard deviation level and the
discount array within a five pip variance.
- The immediate or next logical discount array will be the initial objective.
- There will likely be multiple old daily lows inside the IPA data range, but we will use the one that frames
the potential for at least 50 to 100 Pips.

When we are bullish, we will target the buy side liquidity above an old daily high or fair value gap inside of
the 20-day IPA data range, converging with a standard deviation above (a premium standard deviation).
- The immediate or next logical premium array will be the initial objective.
- There will likely be multiple daily highs inside of the ID data range, but we will use the one that frames the
potential for at least 50 to 100 Pips.

When we are bullish:
- Note the European opening price on Tuesday and filter all longs at or below this price level.
- Anticipate a 15-minute optimal trade entry to form inside of a retracement lower during London open and/
or New York open kill zones, or a sell stop raid.

An alternative bullish trade execution:
- Note the Asian Range High on Tuesday and place a buy stop at this level plus one pip after 2 a.m.
Eastern Standard Time.
- This is essentially buying strength. The assumption is that after 2 a.m., price has already seen a drop.
- Instead of waiting for price to potentially go lower and cause a loss by trying to catch the low, you can use
the Asian Range High, place a buy stop there, and let it buy you into the move with strength.
- This must be done after 2 a.m. When price starts to drop around 2 a.m., if you are still learning to project
the low of the day, you can place your order. Once the market turns around and starts to go higher, you will
buy the breakout of the Asian Range High.
- We anticipate price to have made the low of the day after price starts its decline under the Asian range
low and/or European opening price.

When we are bearish:
- Note the European opening price on Tuesday and filter all shorts at or above this price level.
- Anticipate a 15-minute chart optimal trade entry to form inside of the retracement higher during London
open and/or New York open kill zones, or a buy stop raid.

An alternative bearish trade execution:
- Note the Asian range low on Tuesday and place a sell stop at this level minus one pip after 2 a.m. Eastern
Standard Time.
- We anticipate price to have made a high of the day after price starts its ascent above the Asian Range
High and/or European opening price.

If a setup is not obtained on Tuesday, the alternative trade executions mentioned for both bullish and
bearish scenarios would apply to Wednesday as well. Everything that references Tuesday would be
replaced with Wednesday, and the same process would be repeated using Wednesday's European
opening price.

When entering a short:
- Place a sell limit order on all positions (execute on a demo account).
- Use the standard deviation and PD array convergence minus 5 Pips as your entry price when using the
sell limit order.
- If multiple orders are used, all use the same entry price in the sell limit orders.

When entering a short:
- Place a limit order to take 50 Pips as your objective on one position.
- Place a second limit order to take 75 Pips as your second objective.
- Use multiple orders to manage the trade idea.
- If you capture a 100 pip objective, close the trade and be content.

When entering a short:
- Note the premium array and standard deviation convergence we aim to enter at.
- Place your stop loss above this high plus 25 Pips.
- Do not re-enter if the trade stops out. You can monitor it for experience, but no re-entry is taken; it's one
and done.

When entering a long:
- Place a buy limit order on all positions (execute on a demo account).
- Use standard deviation and PD array convergence plus 5 Pips as your entry price when using the buy
limit order.
- If multiple orders are used, all use the same entry price in the buy limit order.

When entering a long:
- Place a limit order to take 50 Pips out as your objective on one position.
- Place a second limit order to take 75 Pips out as your second objective.
- Use multiple orders to manage the trade idea.
- If you capture a 100 pip objective, collapse the trade and be content.

When entering a long:
- Note the discount array and standard deviation convergence we aim to enter at.
- Place a stop loss below this low minus 25 Pips.
- Do not re-enter if the trade stops out. You can monitor it for experience, but no re-entry is taken; it's one
and done.

As the trade moves into profit, adjust your stop loss:
- When in profit 25 percent of your expected objective, stop loss can be reduced by 25 percent.
- When in profit 50 percent of your expected objective, stop loss can be reduced by 50 percent.
- When the position is at 75 percent of the expected profit objective, stop must be at break even or above it.

Position size calculation formula:
Account Equity Riskpercent / Stop Loss in Pips
- Position size: The amount of leverage your trade assumes.
- Account Equity: The total amount in your trading account.
- Rpercent: The percentage of risk you are willing to take on per trade.
- Stop Loss in Pips: The difference between the entry price and your stop loss, used to divide the result of
the equity multiplied by the risk percentage.

Example:
- Account Equity: $20,000
- Risk per trade: 1.5 percent or $20,000 1.5 percent = $300
- Stop required for the trade: 20 Pips
In micro Lots (1K each is 10 cents per pip):
- 20 Pips 10 cents = $2
- $300 (total risk) / $2 (per pip) = 150 micro Lots per trade (or 1.5 percent of the account equity).
- Always round down.

Example:
- Account Equity: $20,000
- Risk per trade: 1.5 percent or $20,000 1.5 percent = $300
- Stop required for the trade: 20 Pips
In mini Lots (10K each is $1 per pip):
- 20 Pips $1 = $20 (total risk exposure using 20 Pips with $1 per pip leverage)
- $300 (total risk) / $20 = 15 mini Lots per trade (or 1.5 percent risk of the account equity).
- Always round down. Many lots are more flexible.

section_title: STANDARD LOTS AND STOP LOSS ADJUSTMENT
content:
NOTE
Example:
- Account Equity: $20,000
- Risk per trade: 1.5 percent or $20,000 1.5 percent = $300
- Stop required for the trade: 20 Pips
In standard Lots (100K each is $10 per pip):
- 20 Pips $10 = $200 (total risk)
- If total risk assumption is $300 and total risk for one lot is $200, then $300 / $200 = 1.5 standard Lots. You
can only do one lot per trade.
- Always round down.
Note: The model outlined in this trade plan uses a stop loss suggestion of 25 Pips. You would change the
stop required to 25 Pips, and all of the math for position size calculation would adjust accordingly. For
example, changing 20 Pips to 25 Pips in the calculations.
